data.title: MP Materials: The Defense Establishment Comes A-Calling
data.description:
After having clamped down on rear earth element exports earlier this year, China restarted those exports in July after a de-escalation in trade tensions. However, the Chinese government also warned Western companies not to stockpile large inventories as attempts to do so would be met with another prolonged cut-off. Over the last decade, the deterioration in US-China relations has finally led China to make a strategic move that had been years in the making.

In a famous speech in 1992, then-Chinese leader Deng Xiaoping stated: “The Middle East has its oil, China has rare earth: China’s rare earth deposits account for 80 percent of identified global reserves, you can compare the status of these reserves to that of oil in the Middle East: it is of extremely important strategic significance; we must be sure to handle the rare earth issue properly and make the fullest use of our country’s advantage in rare earth resources.”

And during the next decade and a half, China followed Deng’s call to action by ramping rare earth element (“REE”) production to such an extent that by the mid-2000s it was practically the world's only supplier. Furthermore, Chinese producers pursued an integrated approach that included both production and refining.

MP Yesterday

Needless to say, this wasn’t good news for American producers like The Molybdenum Corporation of America, which owned and operated the Mountain Pass rare earth mine in California. Long story short, the mine shut down in 2002, was revived in 2007, put into care and maintenance in 2015, and revived again in 2017 by MP Materials (NYSE:MP).

But after a diplomatic spat with Japan in 2010 resulted in China halting its exports of rare earths to that country, most of the world’s major industrialized nations quickly realized their predicament. This set off a round of exploration and development efforts in numerous mining jurisdictions around the world to restart REE output.

However, even after MP brought Mountain Pass back online in 2017, it wasn’t all lollipops and sunshine as NdPr price swings and strong Chinese production caused MP’s stock price to ebb and flow. But all that now looks like ancient history as recent events have brought much greater attention to this oft-forgotten corner of the metals market. Efforts to foster domestic sourcing have increased as the new US administration has taken a more strident tone in matters of trade and resource independence. Naturally, this has led to a lot of eyes turning to MP Materials and its Mountain Pass property.

MP Today

The Mountain Pass mine is the main domestic source of several REEs and continues to provide decent yields even after 60 years of drilling. At a 2% cut-off, its bastnaesite concentrate provides a TREO (total rare earth oxide) of cerium, lanthanum, neodymium-praseodymium (“NdPr”), samarium, europium, and gadolinium in the following amounts:

As of December 31, 2024, the mine had a Proven Reserve of 810k Short Tons with a grade of 4.06% TREO and a Probable Reserve of 28.88M ST grading 6.02%. It had an Indicated Resource of 4.35M ST grading 3.71% TREO and an Inferred Resource of 13.35M ST with an average grade of 4.79% TREO. The Resource and Reserve are mutually exclusive. The company estimates Life of Mine at 29 years, but the ample Resource leads one to believe that there’s room for LOM extension.

In recent years, MP’s management also made a concerted effort to capture a greater share of the downstream value-add premium for its metal. It appears to be taking a page out of Materion’s (MTRN) playbook and the long-term strategy that company has utilized to dominate the beryllium value chain.

When the company reported Q2 results in early August, management announced that MP had stopped selling concentrate to third parties and would instead stockpile excess production until NdPr oxide refining operations are fully ramped. As can be seen in the exhibit below, the new product categories, coupled with higher REE prices, helped MP show greatly improved numbers over those of corresponding periods last year.

MP Tomorrow

But the company’s Q2 numbers don’t paint the full picture as the march of events promises to shape MP into a very different company within the next few years. In July, the miner signed a deal with Apple (AAPL) to supply the iPhone maker with magnets produced with recycled feedstock; the deal is valued at $500 million.

Now, normally that would be enough; usually, when the CEO of a relatively small mining company signs a sizable deal with the downstream producer of a brand-name product, they will wax lyrically about it forever and a day. For example, when Livent Corp, a lithium miner that was later purchased by Rio Tinto (RIO), signed a long-term $600 million lithium hydroxide supply deal with GM in 2022, the company crowed about it for years.

However, MP’s Apple deal is practically a footnote when compared to its agreement with the DOD. As most readers are probably well aware by now, last month, MP signed a supply deal that will see the Defense Department take an equity stake in the company through the issuance of $400 million of convertible preferred equity and warrants. A full exercise of the warrants and conversion feature would allow the Department of Defense to become the largest shareholder in MP Materials.

Also included is a $150 million low interest loan to fund the expansion of MP’s heavy-rare-earth separation circuit at Mountain Pass. MP will expand its magnet manufacturing capacity by 10X through the construction of a facility that has been dubbed the 10X Facility, and the DOD committed to buy all new production coming from that plant on a cost-plus basis, which includes a $140 million minimum EBITDA guarantee. The deal includes a $110/kg price floor for all products containing NdPr.

Takeaway

Unsurprisingly, MP’s share price has surged since the announcement of both these deals, going from the low-$20 per share range, where the stock traded for most of the first half of the year, to surging as high as the mid-70s in mid-August; MP currently trades at an enterprise value of $12.3 billion. The sharp jump in the share price is enough to give me pause and is the reason why I rate the stock as a Hold.

But what's going on at MP is extremely interesting and definitely merits keeping an eye on. Cooling US-China relations over the last decade are leading the US government to adopt an increasingly active industrial policy. The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 kicked off the process through targeted funding for certain sectors, and the DOD’s purchase of MP Materials’ equity points to an acceleration in this trend. There also appears to be a consensus between both major political parties on this issue.

In my opinion, geopolitical tensions are likely to keep rising; after all, both China and the US want to be the global hegemon, but as the Highlander famously said: There can only be one. This means that I’ll be keeping a close eye on MP’s share price and revisiting the stock on any major pullback.

data.title: Pinpointing Targets In The Financial Sector
data.description:
The Finance sector is ranked as #3 among the 15 we cover for year-ahead returns by the ValuEngine forecast model. Finance sector stocks and ETFs, along with the previously profiled utilities sector, are generally perceived as stodgy stocks that are most commonly bought by older investors for dividend yield. However, that has not been true of either sector recently. The nature of both sectors, finance and utilities, have changed as a result of new technological advancements. The innovations that have affected the financial sector have been more subtle but no less substantial.

This table compares the 11 Select Sector SPDR ETFs. These are among the tools most frequently used by sector rotators, asset allocators and hedge funds to take positions in a specific sector of the economy.

SPLG SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF 3 $82.1 9.86% 15.83% 15.28% 1.2% 17.9

Unsurprisingly, in view of the relentless bull market leadership of technology stocks, the Technology Select Sector SPDR (XLK) is the largest of the group if ranked by assets under management. Performance would be one reason. XLK has outperformed S&P 500 Index ETFs, represented here by SPLG, in all three time periods in this table: year-to-date, 1 year and 5 years. That said, with a VE Rating of 3 (Hold), we expected it to be performing closer to in line with the market during the next one-to-12 months. Two of its top stocks, Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL), are now also ranked 3 (Hold).

On the other hand, two other Select Sector SPDRs with their largest market cap companies also members of the “Magnificent 7” are still rated 5 (Strong Buy). Communications Services (XLC) is bolstered by Google (GOOGL) and Netflix (NFLX), even though Meta Platforms (META) was recently downgraded from 5 to 3. Consumer Discretionary is led by Amazon (AMZN), still rated 5, and Tesla (TSLA), rated 4, in addition to three other Buy or Strong Buy rated stocks among its top 10. Looking at historical performance, neither XLC nor XLY outperformed SPLG (the S&P 500 Index ETF from SSgA) for the past 5-year period. XLY has also significantly underperformed SPLG on a year-to-date basis.

The only Select Sector SPDR that both outperformed in all three time frames and also gets our top rating of 5 for forecasted relative performance is XLF, which represents the Finance sector. It surprised me, and may be somewhat surprising to you, that XLF is the second-largest of the Select Sector SPDR ETFs in total assets - and by a wide margin. With nearly $52 billion, it has more than double the amount of assets in XLC. Using a valuation lens, XLF has a higher dividend yield than XLC, XLK, XLY and SPLG, while being priced at a significantly lower P/E ratio than any of these four.

Eight of the top 10 holdings in XLF are rated 4 (Buy) or 5 (Strong Buy). The Strong Buy stocks are Citigroup (C) and Wells Fargo (WFC). The buy-rated stocks are JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs (GS) and Morgan Stanley (MS). All are US banks or brokerage firms.

In all, 20 financial sector stocks we cover are rated 5 (Strong Buy) in forecasted performance. This table shows all 20. Since they are all top-rated, we ranked this list by our valuation model ranking instead. We also highlighted the four exceptions to a glaringly obvious trend in the names of the companies on the list.

Please note that the four highlighted companies are the only US-domiciled companies on the list. All of the others are stocks foreign-domiciled financial institutions. Furthermore, only 3 stocks are in the top 40% of our valuation model’s rank. The other 17 on the list are classified as between somewhat overvalued to very overvalued. One thing to take into account is that our valuation model is much more sensitive to the current price, with the investor paying for projected future price gains than it is to the historical and often-backward looking ratios commonly favored by many value investors. Accordingly, there tends to be more of a correlation of our value rank to the PEG (Price-to-Earnings Growth) ratio than to Price/Earnings multiples.

We’ve noted a few times since mid-year that the pronounced fall of the value of the dollar relative to the euro has resulted in US-listed American Depository Receipts (ADRs) performing much better during the first half of this year than equivalently sized US companies in the same global industry group. Although the four US-domiciled companies’ stocks performed very well, the top seven 12-month performers were all ADRs of foreign banks. Since the table is ordered with the most undervalued stocks on top, it should not be a surprise that most of the largest gainers in the past 12 months now rank near the bottom of the list for valuation. Nonetheless, our predictive model still predicts all twenty to be well-above average performers in the next 1-to-12 month period. As a bonus, investors in the foreign-domiciled ADRs during the past 12 months also enjoyed a far superior dividend yield.

Although the US-listed ETF we focused on thus far has been the Finance Select Sector XLF, ETFdb.com, our comprehensive source for ETF data from VettaFI has 48 non-leveraged ETFs that cover the sector. 10 of them are leveraged or inverse, which leaves 38 various ways to slice and dice the sector. This includes ETFs that isolate different industries within the sector, such as banking, insurance and capital markets. Other ETFs hold portfolios of the sector globally or in just one region, such as Europe, Asia, Emerging Markets or China. Some also focus on so-called “smart beta” factors such as high-yield and small market capitalization. Here is a snapshot of the top ten performing non-leveraged ones during the past 12 months, excluding ETFs not available for all five years.

The ETF that defines the Financial Sector for many traders, XLF, placed 13th in 1-year price gain. Given our earlier analysis concerning foreign-domiciled large banks, particularly in Europe, it makes sense that the iShares MSCI Europe Financials ETF (EUFN) had the highest gain by a wide margin - nearly 54%, or about 2.5 times the robust 21.7% gained by XLF. That said, a US-only ETF, iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF, IAI, placed second with a 41% price gain. IAI posted at the top for the entire five-year period. EUFN also boasts the highest current dividend yield in this selection, about 3.8%. The third-place finisher targeted only banks. It is the Invesco KBW bank ETF, KBWB, with a 33.3% twelve-month price gain and a yield of 2.2%. Our forecast model expects IAI and KBWB to continue their strong relative performance going forward. Both are rated 5 (Strong Buy). Size is not too much of an issue for most sizes of transactions. KBWB and EUFN also place third and fourth in total assets respectively with $4.6 billion and $4.5 billion.

Both, however, are much smaller than the $52 billion in XLF and the $12 billion in Vanguard Financials ETF, VFH. The last finished 11th overall in price gain. Veteran ETF users will not be surprised to see that the expense ratios for these specialty segment-targeted ETFs are considerably higher than ETFs designed to reflect the entire sector. In fact, there is a huge spread in expense ratios between the three least expensive - XLF, VFH and FNCL from Fidelity, all at just 8 basis points. KBWB, an indexed ETF designed by research firm KBW’s analysts, is at the low end of the higher-expense ratio ETFs at 0.35%. It would normally be expected given foreign custody and analytic data costs that ETFs holding foreign-listed stocks would charge a higher expense ratio. This helps explain why the expense ratio of EUFN is even higher at 0.48%. EUFN still charges less than actively managed DFNL and Global X Fintech ETF FINX with expense ratios above 0.60%.

Conclusion

ValuEngine’s forecast model is very bullish on the Financial sector, its ETFs and an above-average percentage of the stocks we cover. That said, many of the individual stocks and the ETFs that hold them are overvalued according to our valuation model, although not as overvalued as other sectors such as Technology and Consumer Durables. This means that while many of these stocks and ETFs are currently overvalued by ValuEngine, the complex models indicate that they will continue to rise in price further and there is more room for stock or ETF price increases. But the risk is higher than it was last year. The fact that the dividend yields are more generous than those of the major indexes and most other sectors add to the relative attractiveness of the sector.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oshkosh: A Company With Long-Term Reliability But With Short-Term Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My DCF analysis suggests that Oshkosh Corporation (NYSE:OSK) is overvalued by 27%. However, its valuation metrics show that its potential growth is not fully priced in. The company has strong long-term contracts and follows an M&A growth strategy. There are some temporary headwinds for the company, such as the end of the Caterpillar-branded telehandlers contract, which directly affected consolidated sales. I give the stock a Hold rating.

Company Overview

Oshkosh Corporation, which manufactures and distributes special vehicles for different industries worldwide, operates through the following segments: Access, Transport, and Vocational. The Access segment is responsible for manufacturing aerial work and telehandlers for industrial purposes. The Transport segment manufactures tactical vehicles and related services for defense. The Vocational segment provides firefighting equipment and emergency response vehicles. The company was founded in 1917 and is based in Oshkosh, Wisconsin.

As shown in the table below, the company has done multiple acquisitions in recent years, with Hinowa, Oshkosh AeroTech (the renamed entity after the acquisition of the AeroTech business from JBT), and AUSA being the most notable. In March 2023, Oshkosh closed the acquisition of Hinowa. This acquisition offers Oshkosh the opportunity to expand globally in the Access segment. The main reasons for the acquisition, in my view, are the 250,000-square-foot manufacturing facility located in Nogara, Italy, and the opportunity to own the producer of JLG crawler boom lifts.

In August 2023, Oshkosh closed the transaction with JBT that led to the company's AeroTech business. This acquisition is key, as Aerotech's own brands serve around 75% of air travelers at U.S. airports and load around 79% of overnight express packages globally. In September 2024, Oshkosh closed the acquisition of AUSA. AUSA products will complement JLG's, Oshkosh's subsidiary, line of telehandlers. AUSA has worked with JLG in the past, manufacturing the SkyTrak 3013 compact telehander for JLG. These acquisitions are driving Oshkosh's expansion, with AUSA and Hinowa supporting growth in the Access segment and lowering the negative effect on consolidated sales. Oshkosh AeroTech contributed to the segment backlog and consolidated sales performance.

Segment Overview

Access

Sales for the Access segment decreased by 10.7% in Q2 because Oshkosh couldn't produce Caterpillar-branded telehandlers because the agreement between the two firms had expired. However, AUSA's volume sales partially offset this negative effect. This was reflected in the decrease in order backlog of 63.6%, from $3,264.4 million in 2024 to $1,189.0 million in 2025.

Additionally, tariffs may further impact Oshkosh's Access segment, as 407 product categories are cataloged as "derivative" with a 50% tariff on aluminum and steel products. However, the company's execution efforts, such as M&A, are expected to reduce the tariff impact compared to Q1. This segment may be impacted by the previously mentioned factors in Q3 and in the short term. However, its recent acquisitions reinforce its operational focus on long-term growth.

Transport

Segment sales decreased 16.2% in Q2 due to the end of the Joint Light Tactical Vehicle program, though the sales were partly offset by the Next Generation Delivery Vehicle (NGDV) production for the U.S. Postal Service, or USPS. However, the backlog for the segment showed an increase of 5.1% YoY, which means that even after a high-relevance contract has ended, the company has managed to compensate for that by entering into other long-term contracts. The USPS contract is for replacing the "old" Legacy Delivery Mail Vehicle (LLV) with the NGDV. Oshkosh has 51,500 NGDVs on contract that has a potential lifespan of 20 years. With this contract, the company has the opportunity to replace the approximately 130,000 LLVs, with the youngest being 31 years old, with around 165,000 NGDVs.

The macro context for this segment also looks solid in the long term, as it is supported by the federal government's current expenditure. In my view, with these levels of government expenditures, the transport segment can replace all the "old" LLVs.

Vocational

Sales for this segment increased by 15% in Q2 due to volume increases and enhanced pricing. The backlog showed strong growth as it increased 10.4% YoY, from $5,678.2 million to $6,268 million. Oshkosh AeroTech also contributed to this strong performance, as it allowed the company the opportunity to operate in the airport market. With AeroTech, Oshkosh is leveraging its global growth potential in Europe, the Middle East, and Asia. Also, this acquisition has enabled product innovation, such as for the Autonomous Cargo Handler, shared in the investor day presentation.

Takeaway

Despite the expiration of key contracts, the company shows long-term reliability. The Access segment suffered from the expiration of the Caterpillar contract, but recent acquisitions helped to reduce the negative impact partially. The Transport segment saw a decline in sales due to the end of the JLTV program, but this was pegged by the increase in order backlog because of the USPS NGDV contract. The Vocational segment presented positive sales and a backlog, reflecting growth for Oshkosh Aerotech. With its recent acquisitions and new contracts, I believe Oshkosh is in the right position to recover from this drawback.

Quarterly Financial Results

Oshkosh released its Q2 2025 financial results on August 1, delivering strong results overall but with some headwinds.

On the positive side, we have the following:

  • Net income accounted for $204.8 million, a 21.5% YoY increase.
  • EPS increased from $2.56 to $3.16, or up 23.4% YoY.
  • Operating income increased by 11.8%.
  • Corporate and other costs accounted for $55 million, a 47% YOY decrease.

On the negative side:

  • Consolidated sales decreased by 4% year over year
  • The tax rate for pre-tax income accounted for 24.1%, a 0.7% YOY increase

These financial results indicate that profitability is still growing even though sales have decreased. The main reason why consolidated sales fell in the quarter was the bad performance in the Access and Transport segments. Profitability increased due to the 47.12% YoY decrease in corporate and other expenses and the good sales performance in the Vocational segment.

Oshkosh's overall strategy is to grow through mergers and acquisitions. This is reflected in the 10.4% YoY increase in consolidated sales for the Vocational segment, with AeroTech being a key growth contributor.

My outlook for Q3 is bullish despite the sales decline in the Access and Transport segments. The AUSA and Hinowa acquisitions are reducing the negative effects of the Caterpillar contract expiration, and the NGDV USPS contract is driving the order backlog in the Transport business to the positive range. As noted above, government expenditure trends also support the opportunity arising from the USPS contract to replace "old" delivery vehicles. Moreover, long-term contracts offer the company a competitive advantage compared to peers such as Terex (TEX) that lack similar contracts.

Additionally, the Vocational segment has shown strong backlog and sales due to the acquisition of Oshkosh Aerotech, which opened the opportunity to operate in a new market.

Short-Term Headwinds

The main headwind is the expiration of the Caterpillar Telehandler contract, which led to the 63.3% decrease in order backlog in the Access segment. Further, the end of the Joint Light Tactical Vehicle program in the Transport segment led to a 16.2% decline in segment sales. I, however, view these as temporary issues, and I believe the company is in the right position to recover. The recent acquisitions, such as AUSA and Hinowa, are helping to offset the negative effect of the Caterpillar contract expiry, while the USPS contract has the opportunity to replace all the "old" delivery vehicles and has a potential 20-year lifespan.

Tariffs are another short-term headwind for Oshkosh, as they can directly impact its cost of production. However, I believe tariffs will not affect performance compared to Q1 and its acquisitions, plus the USPS contract, are driving performance, as reflected in the increase in order backlogs for the Transport and Vocational segments.

Valuation: DCF Analysis

A DCF analysis shows that Oshkosh is significantly overvalued. I calculated WACC assuming a cost of debt of 7%, a cost of equity of 9.31%, and a capital structure that has 12.16% debt and 87.84% equity. The pre-tax cost of debt was calculated by dividing the interest expense by the total debt. The cost of debt after tax was calculated using this formula: Cost of Debt*(1-Effective Tax Rate). In my model, I expect that the company keeps its current debt levels, or in a similar low-level range.

I calculated the cost of equity using the following formula: (Risk Free Rate)+Beta*(Market Return-Risk Free Rate). The risk-free rate is the yield of the 10Y U.S. Treasury Bonds, and the market return is an estimated average of the S&P500 Index (SP500) over the years. The capital structure breakdown uses the company's market capitalization as equity and total debt as debt, as depicted in the WACC table below.

Moreover, the growth rate used to calculate the FFCF was the rate the construction machinery global market is expected to grow annually. This rate avoids historical volatility and mirrors sector trends. The perpetual growth rate is set to replicate the long-term GDP performance of the U.S. economy to project that the company will keep growing at a moderate rate.

The formula I used to calculate equity value was adding the sum of FCF and the cash on hand and then subtracting it from the total debt. Finally, the DCF price per share was calculated by just dividing the equity value by the shares outstanding.

The fair value derived by my DCF analysis is approximately $109; compared to the current price, this represents a potential overvaluation of around 18.21%.

2028 Financial Targets: Are the numbers achievable?

In June 2025, the company announced its 2028 financial targets. In my view, the revenue target of $13-$14 billion (which is 30.48% above current levels) seems achievable, as the current annual growth rate of 7-8%, which is similar to the CAGR for the construction machinery market, would take the company closer to that figure. This rate mirrors sector trends and provides a conservative estimate of the company's growth. I believe the adjusted EPS target is also achievable, considering this growth estimate.

Competitive Scene

Terex Corporation has the closest brand when compared to Oshkosh's Access segment. As shown below, both companies have delivered a similar EBITDA margin over the years. Oshkosh's competitive advantage is its long-term contracts, such as the NGDV program, which makes the company strongly reliable in the long term and highly profitable as well (as was shown in its Q2 performance).

Terex doesn't have a segment or any products similar to Oshkosh's Transport and Vocational segments. This indicates that Oshkosh has more diversified revenue streams with strategic positioning and the ability to win key contracts.

According to a Business Research Company study, the global construction machinery market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 8.1% from 2024 to 2029. Oshkosh maintains its profitability due to its long-term contracts and its M&A strategy. I estimate that EBITDA margin will remain above 10% in the long term if the company continues to deliver on profitability.

As can be seen from the table below, Oshkosh ranks high vs. peers on key metrics even as its valuation is low compared to the sector median. This shows that Oshkosh's future potential growth isn't priced in.

The table below represents the profitability metrics for Oshkosh and Terex. Oshkosh presents more steady profitability metrics than Terex. Both companies have similar gross profit margins, but Oshkosh's net income margin is twice as big as Terex. This means that even as both companies have a similar level of production efficiency, Oshkosh can generate double the profitability of its competitor.

Risks

The company is highly sensitive to industry and economic trends. In the event of a downturn, Oshkosh would be affected negatively, which can reflect on its share price too.

Another area of concern is its dependency on contracts. For example, the end of the Caterpillar-branded telehander contract directly affected sales in the Access segment. Additionally, the company depends on government programs such as the Joint Light Tactical Vehicle and the Next Generation Vehicle production program. This dependency was reflected in Oshkosh's consolidated sales. These risks may negatively impact Oshkosh in Q3, but still, my expectations for the next quarter are solid.

The third area of concern is tariffs on steel products, as they may impact the production cost of Oshkosh's products. However, the company expects a lower impact in 2025 compared to the previous quarter.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my DCF-derived price per share suggests the OSK stock is significantly overvalued, but it offers long-term reliability. The company suffers from different short-term headwinds, such as the decline in the Access segment backlog, the expiration of key contracts, and tariffs that may impact the cost of its products. However, contracts such as the USPS NGDV contract and recent acquisitions highlight long-term potential, and so I assign a Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Simon Property Group Is Deftly Handling Changing Tastes
data.description:
Simon Property Group, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:SPG) has underperformed the market YTD, including with high single-digit underperformance since we last recommended against investing. Despite that, the company is intelligently tackling a new wave of modern malls, with an impressive portfolio of assets, and the ability to continue driving strong returns.

Simon Property Group Q2 2025 Results

Simon Property Group earned more than $500 million, substantial YoY growth.

The nature of depreciation means that funds from operations, or FFO, is much more important. The company had almost $1.2 billion in FFO, which is what enables the company to drive strong dividends. Versus a $56 billion valuation, that's an almost 9% FFO yield which enables the company to comfortably pay its almost 5% dividend yield.

The company's strong portfolio has enabled it to maintain strong occupancy despite a shift away from malls. The company's at 96% occupancy a 4% increase YoY and the company has also managed to increase its base minimum rent per square foot by 1.3%. Retailer sales per square foot have remained strong which is key to the company continuing rent.

The company increased its dividend by 4.9% YoY, which is impressive given the company's current almost 5% dividend yield.

Simon Property Group Breakdown

Looking at the breakdown, the company's financials remain quite strong and enable it to take advantage of new opportunities.

The company's share price weakness has pushed its valuation to below $70 billion, and the company has minimum preferred equity or any dilution. The company has also not substantially diluted shareholders, which is good to see for an REIT. The company's total revenue has increased YoY and the company's FFO from the operating partnership has enabled the company to grow.

It's impressive to see the company with a mid-single-digit dividend yield and the ability to continue increasing it at a mid-single-digit %.

The company has an incredibly diversified portfolio. Simon Property Group is centered around U.S. malls and premium outlets, and the company is diversified across dozens of states. Almost 20% of the company's revenue comes from Florida, but Florida is a diverse market with a wide range major cities.

The company's diversification means there's minimal geographical risk in our view.

Simon Property Group Financial Breakdown

The company has a diverse portfolio of assets and its rent expirations enable it to have years of stable revenue.

The company has minimal turnover in its anchor leases which are a small % of revenue but a large % of the company's square foot. Still most of these leases go into the 2030s. The company's more valuable renting properties that drive the high rents have valuable leases especially going into the mid-2030s. The company's minimal turnover per year enables it to handle a market downturn.

The company's net debt is sub $30 billion and more than $10 billion in non-recourse mortgage rate. The company's fixed charge coverage is more than enough for the company to handle the current higher interest rates and the company's almost 7 weighted average years to maturity enable it to handle a batch of higher interest rates.

Simon Property Group has done a great job of managing its average interest rate at sub 4% in an expensive market, and with interest rates expected to decline, we don't see any major interest risks for the company. The FFO payout ratio of <70% enables the company to continue paying a strong dividend and investing in its business.

The company has premium properties.

Stanford shopping mall is so busy that users are complaining about how crowded it is. In Pal Alto where it's located, the city's tax revenue has nearly recovered to pre-pandemic levels. That's because the company is benefiting from the rise of third spaces as malls, building elegant modern malls for its businesses.

We expect that to continue to be reflected in the company's future rent recapture.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is another black-swan event. COVID-19 was a black swan event that substantially declined mall traffic and was a major impact and existential threat for Simon Property Group. Another unexpected black swan event could hurt the company's ability to continue its shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Simon Property Group has an impressive portfolio of mall assets as seen with the rise of malls as third spaces. The company has continued to generate substantial FFO, and it's working to increase that FFO. The company offers a mid-single-digit dividend yield and it's continuing to increase that yield by the mid-single-digits.

The company has a diverse portfolio of assets across numerous markets in both the United States and internationally. The company's rent due is manageable and spread across a number of years. Going forward, we expect the company to continue generating strong and incredibly reliable returns, making it a valuable investment.

data.title: GATX Corporation: Still Not Hitching My Future To This Name
data.description:
In May 2024, one company that I decided to rate a "Hold" was GATX Corporation (GATX). The business, which owns a large fleet of rail cars, is certainly the kind of firm that interests me. Having said that, while the overall trend of financial performance for the business was decent, shares were not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. Whenever I rate a company a "Hold," I'm making the claim that shares are likely to perform more or less similarly to the broader market for the foreseeable future. And I would say we have had that. Since the time that the article was published, the stock has been up 26.1%. That's a bit above the 23.1% increase seen by the S&P 500 over the same period. But considering how much time has passed, I wouldn't call it a significant disparity.

Looking at the picture today, I see that the firm is cheaper on a forward basis than it was back then. But between how it is priced on an absolute basis and how it is priced compared to other similar enterprises, I'm not ready to upgrade it at this time. This is even after management announced earlier this year a rather large investment that should, in the long run, bode well for shareholders. Given these thoughts, I believe that maintaining the company as a "Hold" candidate is the right choice at this time.

Taking a ride on GATX

Given how much time has elapsed, I think it would be helpful to dig a bit into the overall core operations of GATX. According to management, the company leases, operates, manages, and re-markets certain rail-related assets. It is a pretty large firm. At the end of the most recent quarter, which should be the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, its Rail North America fleet consisted of 102,317 railcars. These boasted a 99.2% utilization rate. That's about as high as you can get.

This does not include boxcars the company does not factor them into these members. Those totaled 7,621 at the end of the most recent quarter. The reason why management does not include these in the other count is simply because they seem to be in the process of gradually phasing out this part of the business. A year earlier, for instance, the company had 8,990 boxcars to its name. But since then, it has scrapped a total of 1,361 while selling off another 35. It added only 27 during this time. For its international operations, which involve its business activities in Europe and India, the business has 30,492 railcars.

There is yet a third operating segment that the company has. But this is quite small in the grand scheme of things. For context, during the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, this segment, known as Engine Leasing, accounted for only 3.4% of the firm's overall revenue, so it barely deserves a mention. The only reason why I discuss it, however, is that it generates a lot of profitability. In the first half of this year, it was responsible for 20.5% of total segment profits. This part of the business consists of certain joint venture ownerships, including a manufacturer of commercial aircraft engines.

Over the last couple of years, we have seen solid growth for the business. In 2024, revenue was $1.59 billion. That's up from the $1.41 billion the company reported the previous year. This was driven by growth across all three of its operating segments.

Rail North America, for instance, saw revenue grow 11.8% from $982.7 million to $1.10 billion. This was thanks to a jump in lease revenue, with management attributing this to a combination of higher lease rates and more railcars on lease. It appears as though most of the change would have had to have been from higher rates. I say this because the number of railcars that were active on an average basis during the year 2024 came in only 1.2% above what the company saw the previous year.

This was not all. For its international operations, the company saw revenue expand from $309.5 million to $350.3 million. An increase in the average active rail car of 1.7% certainly helped. But obviously, higher rates did the heavy lifting. And finally, the Engine Leasing segment reported a rise in revenue from $77.2 million to $97.1 million. This was entirely because non-dedicated engine revenue rose in response to aircraft spare engines acquired during the year.

With revenue rising, profitability for the business also increased. Net income grew from $259.2 million to $284.2 million. We saw operating cash flow jump from $520.4 million to $602.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $526.8 million to $603 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $782.6 million to $899.1 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, we have results for the first two quarters. An increase in average active rail cars and higher rates pushed revenue up for the Rail North America segment to $589 million compared to the $535.4 million the company reported the previous year. That was instrumental in pushing overall revenue for the company up from $766.6 million in the first half of 2024 to $852.1 million at the same time this year.

In its international business, GATX reported a rise in revenue from $168.9 million to $184.3 million. This came as the average active rail car has grown slightly from 28,198 to 28,572. But again, higher lease rates accounted for the lion’s share of the growth. There was also some growth when it came to the Engine Leasing segment, with sales climbing from $43.1 million to $58.2 million. All of this seems to have stemmed from aircraft spare engines acquired last year that were utilized in the engine capacity agreement that the company has with Rolls-Royce.

Net income for the company also jumped, rising from $118.7 million to $154.1 million. We saw operating cash flow expand from $235.9 million to $285.5 million. On an adjusted basis, the increase was from $251.7 million to $292 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $430.2 million to $494.1 million. For the current fiscal year, as a whole, management expects earnings per share of between $8.50 and $8.90. At the midpoint, we would be looking at net profits of $312.3 million compared to the $284.2 million reported for 2024. It's also looking like adjusted operating cash flow will be somewhere around $699.5 million, while EBITDA will be around $1.03 billion.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management made a rather interesting maneuver earlier this year. Specifically, this was the firm's decision to partner up with institutional firms, namely Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP), that involve a joint venture formed between the parties that will then subsequently acquire the operating lease portfolio of railcars currently owned by a subsidiary of Wells Fargo & Company (WFC). This is a massive transaction with a value of $4.4 billion.

Initially, ownership interest in this joint venture will be 30% for GATX and 70% for Brookfield. However, GATX will manage the railcars of the joint venture, as well as all railcars and locomotives wholly owned by Brookfield. The details are rather complex, but in short, over time, GATX has the opportunity to acquire up to 100% of the joint venture.

The structure of all of this is rather intriguing. For instance, 70% of the purchase price of the venture will come from committed debt financing of the joint venture which will unfortunately be guaranteed by GATX. The other 30% will be paid for through the two enterprises putting in $1.3 billion in exchange for equity in it.

This deal is not likely to close until early next year. Unfortunately, we don't really have much in the way of further information to gauge how profitable it might be. We do know that third-party estimates peg the growth in annual EBITDA associated with this maneuver as amounting to between 10% and 15% for the business. But because we don't have more concrete figures than this, I think it would be best to value the company excluding the prospect of growth here.

In the chart above, you can see how shares of the company are currently priced using estimates for 2025 and historical results for 2024. On an absolute basis, the stock looks decent but far from great. In the table below, I then compared it to four other similar enterprises. What I found was that, on a price-to-earnings basis, two of the four companies ended up being cheaper. This number increases to three of the four companies on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis. But when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, our candidate was the most expensive of the group.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
GATX Corporation 18.3 8.1 13.4
Trinity Industries, Inc. (TRN) 24.5 5.6 11.7
The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX) 6.5 4.0 6.1
Wabash National Corporation (WNC) 5.9 4.1 4.4
FreightCar America, Inc. (RAIL) 978.0 8.3 6.3

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I believe that GATX is an interesting business. But it's not one that I am particularly enthusiastic about from an investment perspective. Shares are decently priced, but far from cheap. The stock actually looks a bit expensive compared to other similar firms. Yes, management is achieving nice growth, but it's not enough, in my opinion, to justify a bullish outlook at this time. Therefore, I have no problem keeping it rated a "Hold" for now.

data.title: Woori Financial: Stay The Course On Korea's Banking Laggard
data.description:
Woori Financial Group Inc. (NYSE:WF) is one of Korea’s ‘big four’ financial holding companies, with subsidiaries spanning Woori Card, Woori Bank, Woori Asset Management, Woori Investment Securities, and, most recently, life insurance (via the Tong Yang and ABL acquisitions). Since I last covered the name, Woori has continued its scorching year-to-date rally, along with the rest of Korea’s outperforming banking sector.

That said, the sector-wide rally has been somewhat interrupted in recent weeks by factors beyond Woori’s control. Namely, slightly disappointing (but still much improved) tax reforms by the new government and potential new regulatory fines. Plus, a key tenet of the Woori thesis that I have written about previously, the stock’s ‘deep value’, isn’t necessarily as compelling now following this year’s massive re-rate.

The key question then, now that Q2 earnings are done and two non-bank subsidiaries have been bolted on, is how much upside (if any) remains for Woori at these levels.

Defending Margins in the Face of Interest Rate Cuts

Korean interest rates are on the decline, so Woori’s resilient Q2 2025 numbers were a very positive outcome. In fact, the core banking business even saw some net interest margin expansion (+1bp for bank and card). On the revenue side, loan growth picked up thanks to an accelerating retail segment, which more than offset weakness in corporates.

Woori also tapped into cost levers to defend its margin – excluding one-offs, operating expenses were down 10.2% for the quarter. The only blemish for Woori Bank was credit costs, which rose to 49 bps on “preemptive provisioning for completion guaranteed trust projects”. Excluding this one-off, credit cost would have held firm. With management also guiding toward full-year credit costs staying within the low-to-mid 40 bps range, things look manageable enough.

The big headline positive for earnings, though, was non-interest income. Thanks to some very strong market conditions in Korea, as well as FX translation gains during the quarter, Woori saw a +47% quarter-on-quarter gain. Still, it’s important to keep in mind that most of this is non-recurring; instead, I’d focus on the resilience of Woori's core bank margins as the key positive takeaway.

Better Capitalized for Shareholder Returns

Perhaps the bigger positive, though, was that Woori is making good on its promise for 2025 to be “the year of capital ratio improvement”. Helped by a resilient P&L (+40 bps) and, to a lesser extent, FX (+15 bps), the group’s CET1 ratio improved to 12.76%. For context, this is above management’s 12.5% full-year target (post dividend) and puts Woori well on the path to reaching 13.0% well ahead of its end-2027 target.

A better capitalized balance sheet is important because it adds room for a big step up in capital returns (dividend + buybacks). The intent from management has also been positive, with both Woori’s dividend (+11%) and buybacks (+10%) already tracking well above last year’s “record high level”.

As for the longer term, management has already targeted a higher dividend payout ratio of 30%, as well as increased share buybacks/cancellations as part of its 50% “mid to long-term total shareholder return target”. All in all, a good setup for Woori’s next “corporate value enhancement” update.

Tax Reforms Boost Dividend Appeal

Externally, the new Korean government’s effort to reform the tax code also represents a tailwind for high-yielding Woori (~5% trailing dividend + high-single digits including buybacks).

While not quite on par with the market’s optimism (hence, Woori’s consolidation in recent weeks), the proposed revision still brings the top-end dividend income tax rate down to 35% for incomes above KRW300m - ten points off the current tax levy of 15-45%.

In addition to a lower tax burden, this amendment also breaks out dividend income from a catch-all “comprehensive income tax”. The caveat is that companies also need to meet a “high-dividend” classification, which entails having a payout ratio of 25% and a +5% rise in the cash dividend vs the trailing three-year average, to qualify. Not too many Korean corporates meet this threshold, so those that do, like Woori (~29% payout in FY24), stand to benefit disproportionately.

Non-Bank Catalyst in the Pipeline

Woori’s earnings have historically been driven by its core banking business, but management is actively diversifying into non-bank income streams. Firstly, from the brokerage business, which got up and running in H1; secondly, from two life insurance acquisitions, Tongyang and ABL Life, both of which are now incorporated as subsidiaries (as of July).

Financially, management is still “reevaluating all assets and liabilities held by the two companies at fair value”, so both insurers’ earnings have yet to kick in. The strategic direction of both businesses (“whether it will be merged or whether we will have 100%-owned subsidiaries”) hasn’t yet been decided either.

We are due for some kind of guidance, though, in terms of both the financial impact and business strategy, with earnings set to kick in from H2 2025. Expect this to catalyze upward revisions to Street estimates in the coming months.

As for the longer term, non-bank accretion really depends on the extent to which these businesses (brokerage + life insurance) lift Woori’s return on equity (or ROE) profile. Insurance, for instance, looks set to boost earnings but will also come with integration costs and “some impact” on capitalization levels.

The good news for investors is that the bar is very low. At ~0.5x forward book (~0.6x trailing) against a ~9% ‘steady state’ ROE for legacy Woori and a 10% “mid to long-term” target, the market isn’t pricing in very much non-bank success at all. Thus, any evidence of an ROE uplift could re-rate the stock quite significantly.

Summing Up

The easiest money might have already been made now that Woori has rallied over 60% this year, but a review of its fundamentals suggests there’s still upside potential – especially with the stock priced at only half of its forward book. In the meantime, keep an eye out for catalysts on the non-bank side, regulatory developments, and shareholder returns. All of these could catalyze a further narrowing of Woori’s deep valuation discount.

data.title: Genesco Cannot Grow Profitably, And Yet Trades At 20x Earnings
data.description:
Genesco Inc. (NYSE:GCO) recently reported Q2 '26 (calendar 2Q25) results. The results were not good, with revenue comps up MSD, but gross margins deteriorating and larger operating losses than a year ago.

I continue to believe the name is overvalued, trading at almost $30, versus an earnings guidance for the year of $1.50. 20x for a retailer in this context is simply too expensive. I maintain a Hold.

Q2 '26 results

Top line still positive: Genesco posted its 4th consecutive quarter of top-line positive comps (at 4%).

In particular, Journeys grew 9% comps, with the company commenting on the call that it is growing double digits in early Q3 '26 (Q3 '25 calendar). The brand is growing on top of growth, because Journeys also grew DD in Q3 '25 (Q3 '24 calendar).

Schuh, on the other hand, remains very challenged, with comps down 4%, despite higher markdowns and promotions.

The cost of growth: The problem with the above growth is that gross margins are down 100bps, because of markdowns and more promotionality.

The company's sales increased $20 million, but gross profits increased only $5 million, or 25% on the margin, versus gross margins of 46% for the quarter. That means the company's new sales (or the aggregate of sales) are much less profitable than the company's regular sales.

SG&A delever on a gross basis: Gross profits increased by $5 million, but SG&A increased by $10 million. The figure levered against sales by barely 20 bps, but in reality, it delevered against business profitability.

SG&A should have leveraged more because it holds the occupancy expenses, potentially the most leverageable part of a retailer's cost structure. However, the company spent more on marketing.

Operating income is worse than last year: What is surprising about the results is that despite the revenue growth, the company lost more money operationally than last year, both on a Q2 '26 or H1 '26 basis.

In fact, for Q2 '26, operating losses widened by 50%, from $10 million to almost $15 million.

The idea I get from all of this is that the company is buying sales with more promotions and higher advertising expenses. This is good for results releases showing growth, but it doesn't bring home more profitability.

Hopes for H2 '26

The company maintained guidance for EPS at $1.3/1.7 for the year. Given it has generated losses of more than $40 million in H1 '26, it means it expects H2 '26, in particular the key Back To School and Holidays seasons, to deliver the profitability for the year. $1.7 in EPS implies a net profit of $18 million, or basically more than $60 million in operating income in H2 '26.

Valuation is too high

Genesco is growing MSD, which is growth but not impressive, and shows signs that such growth cannot be done profitably, as promotions and advertising eat those profits. Indeed, growth leads to lower (negative) operating margins.

A company that cannot grow profits should not command a high earnings multiple. However, Genesco currently trades at almost $30 but is guiding for EPS of $1.5 for the year, which implies a 20x earnings multiple.

Not only this, there are so far no signs that the guidance is achievable as the company is not leveraging, and may need to over-discount in the key holiday sales seasons.

I believe the name is clearly not an opportunity and can even be considered overvalued. For this reason, I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: SPS Commerce: Victim Of Trade Uncertainty
data.description:
In June, I believed that SPS Commerce (NASDAQ:SPSC) suffered from a slower pace of growth, creating a more compelling situation. Following a 25% pullback observed at the time, I was growing more compelled to the shares, but did not see appeal.

This came as much of the growth was driven by M&A efforts and adjusted for stock-based compensation expenses, earnings were not that impressive, as I concluded to revisit this stance if shares would move closer to the $100 mark.

A $140 stock at the time has fallen another 20% over the summer, moving down on the back of the announcement of the second quarter earnings report and comments about its outlook, making it opportune to revisit the investment case here.

Electronic Data Integration

SPS Commerce is a provider of EDI solutions, with EDI standing for Electronic Data Integration, used for electronic data integration between trading partners and stakeholders across supply chains operated by retailers and other (retail) groups.

The idea is to share information to facilitate quick delivery, optimal planning, logistics, and inventory management, all aimed to minimize the time and maximize the efficiency of the route traveled from producers to shelf.

The company provides such services to more than 100,000 companies, offering various functions related to fulfillment, compliance and others. A $150 million business in the mid 2010s has increased sales by a factor of 5 times to about three quarters of a billion, while the organic and inorganic growth story came at the expense of "only" 15% dilution, while real margin gains were delivered upon.

Boosting M&A

SPS Commerce has been quite acquisitive over the past year, including a $206 million purchase of SupplyPike in the summer of last year, an expensive deal with the automated invoice deduction management software business generating just $25 million in annual sales. The company furthermore announced a $210 million deal to acquire Carbon6 Technologies early in 2024, with the company providing software solutions to Amazon sellers, which came at a lower sales multiple, with the revenue contribution pegged at $40 million, for a combined $65 million revenue contribution supporting current sales growth.

In February of this year, the company reported a 19% increase in 2024 sales to $638 million, with GAAP operating profits reported at $89 million, this number was impacted by a $23 million amortization charge. For the year, GAAP earnings were posted at $2.04 per share, with adjusted earnings reported at $3.48 per share, yet as much of the discrepancy stemmed from a $54 million stock-based compensation expense, realistic earnings only trending around $2.50 per share.

For the year 2025, the company guided for sales to increase between 19-20% to $758-$763 million, with adjusted earnings seen between $3.78-$3.84 per share. This $0.33 per share improvement in adjusted earnings looks promising, yet stock-based compensation were seen increasing to $63 million as well, which really suggests that if we take this expense out, earnings would really be stagnant.

In April, the company reported a solid 21% increase in first quarter sales to $181 million, with adjusted earnings reported at $1.00 per share, with full-year earnings now seen between $3.86 and $3.93 per share, with realistic earnings seen around $3 per share if we back out stock-based compensation expenses. Trading at $140, the company was valued at $5.3 billion, a valuation including a $95 million net cash position.

At such levels, multiples were still demanding at $140, for an earnings multiple in the mid-forties. This was a too demanding valuation multiple in my view given that half of the 20% growth as the result of M&A efforts, as growth was slowing down, also due to trade complications induced by tariffs. With valuations being too demanding, I was hoping for a continuation of growth and/or pullback to reconsider this stance.

Coming Down

After trading around the $140 mark in June, shares plunged to levels seen near the $100 mark by the end of July, upon the release of the second quarter earnings report. Shares hit a low of $102 per share and ever since have recovered to $111 per share at this moment of writing.

Second quarter sales grew as much as 22% to $187 million, a slight acceleration in the pace of growth, aided by the purchase of Carbon6 which closed in February, with adjusted earnings reported at $1.00 per share, in line with the first quarter. GAAP earnings only came in at $0.52 per share. Stock-based compensation expense this quarter totaled $15 million on a pre-tax basis, or about $0.30 per share if we assume statutory tax rates, for realistic earnings around $0.70 per quarter, or $2.80 per share for the year.

In this light, the third quarter guidance felt a bit soft, with sales seen up 17-18% to $192-$193 million, with adjusted earnings seen between $0.96-$1.00 per share, with stock-based compensation expenses furthermore seen up to $16 million for the quarter. For the year, sales are seen around $761 million, with adjusted earnings at the midpoint seen topping $4 per share.

Trading down to $111, the market value of the firm has fallen to $4.27 billion, including a $107 million net cash position, for a $4.16 billion operating asset valuation. Based on the full-year outlook, this valued the business just over 5 times sales here, and with cash holdings equal to about $3 per share, unleveraged assets trade at a realistic 38 times earnings multiple based on realistic earnings power around $2.80 per share.

What Now?

Frankly, the lower pace of growth does not surprise me given the macroeconomic economic conditions, as the high multiple left shares vulnerable, as all that exactly happened over the summer. Perhaps some investors were betting on a more muted impact amidst macro uncertainty, as the solutions of SPS Commerce might be even more valuable in choppy trading times, yet its customers are clearly not in spending/investment mode right now.

Right now, earnings multiples have come in from about 45 times to 38 times, largely driven by the multiple coming in, which is still a high multiple, all while realistic earnings are a bit lower than I anticipated as well. Amidst all this, investors are fearful, amidst an uncertain trading environment, slower growth, and higher stock-based compensation.

Another concern is that of comments from management on the conference call that revenue growth is seen coming down to high single digits beyond 2025, being somewhat of a warning into 2026. This might have overshadowed comments that the company sees room for EBITDA margins to increase by another 2 points per annum, which frankly looks quite ambitious and encouraging.

In between all this, multiples have come in and appeal has improved, yet that's still not the same as appeal is to be seen, as I'm lowering my desired entry target to levels sub-$100, as I look forward with greater interest to keep an eye on the shares here.

data.title: Boeing 737 MAX Nears Key Milestone
data.description:
The Boeing Company (NYSE:BA) has strong momentum this year with a revival of order inflow and production, as I discussed in a prior report. According to a news item by Seeking Alpha, the company indicated that it will raise the production of the best-selling Boeing 737 to 42 airplanes per month, effectively lifting the FAA cap that did not allow production to increase above 38 airplanes per month. In this report, I will discuss what a production rate increase to 42 airplanes per month means for Boeing in terms of ramping up production and revenues.

Before I run my analysis, I want to summarize several items from a prior report on the Boeing 737 MAX topic:

  • Boeing’s backlog comfortably supports production rates of 63 airplanes per month without any order inflow.
  • With a fourth line to be installed in Everett, the company will have an annual production capacity for the Boeing 737 MAX of 84 airplanes per month.
  • In the absence of a rate hike this year, realizing year-on-year growth on program revenues would be challenging.
  • A rate hike by mid-year would have resulted in a 9% growth in revenues in 2026.

These assumptions will be revised as needed. I also adjusted the rate ramp-up for the baseline assumption, eliminating a possible gap between increases and production that was too harsh.
